title: The Information Bottleneck Method
venue_name: Proceedings of the 37th Annual Allerton Conference on Communication, Control, and Computing
abstract: We define the relevant information in a signal $x \in X$ as being the information that this signal provides about another signal $y \in Y$. Examples include the information that face images provide about the names of the people portrayed, or the information that speech sounds provide about the words spoken. Understanding the signal $x$ requires more than just predicting $y$, it also requires specifying which features of $X$ play a role in the prediction. We formalize this problem as that of finding a short code for $X$ that preserves the maximum information about $Y$.
